entry.title: Re: How to Configure Alert/Notification from BizX To‑Do for E‑Learning Courses?
entry.content:
Hi @JyotiA 

Regarding your requirements:

  • When an e‑learning course is assigned to a user, a To‑Do task in BizX should be created --> that's already expected to happen. For required entities, with required dates, a Learning Assignment card should be created in the user's BizX homepage (Learning on the Home Page | SAP Help Portal). If that's not happening in your company's tenant, please raise a ticket to the 'LOD-SF-LMS-TIL' component.
  • The user should receive a notification/alert (email or system notification) prompting them to complete the assigned course --> Learning Plan notifications can be automatically sent, if the proper settings and respective automatic process is enabled in LMS (https://me.sap.com/notes/0002184826).
  • Ideally, reminders should also be triggered based on due dates (for example, upcoming due date or overdue status) --> same as above: depending on the settings and if the Learning Expiration automatic process is enabled in LMS, users should be notified of their pending assignments (https://me.sap.com/notes/0003532489).

Now regarding your questions:

  1. Is it possible to create or synchronize BizX To‑Do tasks based on Learning course assignments? --> From BizX, nudge notifications can be triggered by managers for their team members as per KBA https://me.sap.com/notes/2089551. However, this needs to be done manually, card by card. There doesn't seem to be a way to automate such notifications.
  2. Which configuration areas are involved (for example, Learning notifications, Intelligent Services, or BizX integrations)? --> N/A
  3. Are there any standard KBA, guides, or best practices for this setup? --> See above.
  4. Any known limitations or prerequisites that should be considered? --> For the cards to be created and displayed in BizX, please refer to Learning on the Home Page | SAP Help Portal. Regarding other topics mentioned above, please review the documentation shared above. If any questions, please let me know.

Best regards,

entry.title: Let’s Talk SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity: Landscape, Onboarding Process and Roles
entry.content:
Connecting your ERP system to multiple banks can be complex—but with SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity (MBC), organizations gain a centralized, secure, and efficient way to manage bank communications.

In this blog, we walk you through:

  • The architecture and landscape
  • The onboarding process
  • The roles and responsibilities involved in a successful integration

 

Why SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity?

 

The SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity connector acts as the central communication hub between your ERP system and banks or financial institutions.

Every message exchanged—payments, statements, etc.—flows through this connector, ensuring:

  • Full transparency of bank communication
  • Real-time updates of payment status and cash positions
  • End-to-end traceability using the Manage Bank Messages SAP Fiori app (SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition)

Every message sent through this connector includes key details like sender and receiver IDs, message type, file name, and message content. Additionally, it contains important context information such as Swift parameters or approval user details, which are essential for seamless bank interactions.

 

System Landscape Overview

Using SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity truly enhances your control, efficiency, and transparency in financial accounting. This solution automatically updates payment statuses and cash positions in the ERP system as soon as new information is available from the banks.

SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity has two main environments: test and production.

It's important to connect these to the matching systems—test-to-test and production-to-production—on the customer side. These test and production systems are kept separate for safety and further protected by different security measures.

In the SAP BTP environment, SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity is a managed service. This means the subaccounts won't be visible under the customer's global accounts in BTP. Still, customers can easily access the SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity applications to set up and monitor their ERP connections.

There are two connections that need to be setup when you get onboarded to SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity: the first from your ERP system to SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity, and the second from SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity to your banks or FSIs.

 

Onboarding Process – End-to-End Journey

The onboarding process is structured into several key steps to ensure a smooth and secure integration:

1. Configure Test ERP and Connect to MBC Test Tenant

Set up your ERP test system and establish the connection with the SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity (MBC) test tenant. Ensure that you have the required authorizations to access MBC applications.

2. Configure MBC Test Tenant and Connect to Bank

Complete the configuration of your MBC test tenant. The SAP MBC team will then establish the connection between your tenant and the respective banks or financial service institutions (FSIs).

3. Perform End-to-End Testing

Execute comprehensive end-to-end testing to validate the entire integration flow before proceeding to the production environment.

4. Configure Production ERP and Connect to MBC Production Tenant

Set up your production ERP system and integrate it with the MBC production tenant. Verify that access to all required MBC applications is properly assigned.

5. Configure MBC Production Tenant and Connect to Bank

Finalize the configuration of the production MBC tenant. The SAP MBC team will connect it to your banks or FSIs.

6. Execute Penny Testing

Perform penny testing in the production environment to confirm that transactions are successfully processed between the ERP system and the banks.

7. Production Support and Maintenance

After going live, follow standard SAP support procedures for any issues or inquiries.

For configuration changes, submit an additional request for existing connections
To onboard new banks, refer to the relevant FAQ on adding banks to an existing SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity setup

 

Roles and Responsibilities

A successful MBC implementation depends on strong collaboration between SAP and the customer.

SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity Team:

  • Activated after service request submission
  • Establishes connectivity between MBC and banks
  • Resolves technical issues
  • Supports tenant troubleshooting

Customer:

The customer plays the leading role across the entire journey:

  • Provisions MBC tenants
  • Configures ERP-to-MBC authentication
  • Coordinates with banks and Swift
  • Submits service requests
  • Drives testing and validation
  • Owns contractual agreements with banks
  • Provides technical expertise internally
  • Acts as an intermediary with third-party institutions

Cloud Administrator (IT Contact):

  • Main contact for onboarding
  • Receives provisioning and onboarding emails
  • Provisions tenants
  • Creates and manages service requests
  • Assigns authorizations

Super Administrator:

  • Broader visibility across systems
  • More relevant for on-premise scenarios
  • Can also provision tenants and manage service requests

Resources:

  • SAP Help Portal: SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity 
  • Onboarding Resource Center 
  • SAP Help Portal – MBC Frequently Questions

entry.title: Re: Week 3 - Use AI Agents with Grounding Tools
Role Management Strategy in EWM Implementation Project (Build Phase vs Production)
entry.content:
Hi Experts,
I am currently working on an EWM implementation project and need some guidance on role management during the build phase.
In the build phase, we are creating separate roles for each module. I have a few questions regarding how to handle these roles across systems:
Should these build-phase roles be transported to the Production system, or should they remain only in DEV and QA systems?
If roles are maintained only in DEV and QA, then during system refresh activities, these roles might get deleted. How is this typically handled?
If roles are moved to Production, it may result in many unassigned roles, increasing clutter. Is this acceptable or is there a better way to manage this?
Could you please suggest the best practice for role management in an implementation project, especially for SAP EWM?
Thanks in advance!

entry.title: SAP Developer News for April 30th, 2026
entry.content:
Description

🚨 NPM supply chain attack campaign, SAP packages affected

  • Step Security post https://www.stepsecurity.io/blog/a-mini-shai-hulud-has-appeared

CAP Apr 2026 release

  • Release notes https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/releases/2026/apr26

The refreshed SAP Architecture Center

  • Announcement: https://architecture.learning.sap.com/news/2026/04/22/introducing-the-refreshed-sap-architecture-center
  • SAP Architecture Center: https://architecture.learning.sap.com/

CAP AI Plugin for Node.js

  • GitHub Repo: https://github.com/cap-js/ai

DYK #14 CW18

  • More Info: https://help.sap.com/docs/build-apps/service-guide/trigger-event

CHAPTER TITLES

0:00 Intro

0:10 🚨 NPM supply chain attack campaign, SAP packages affected

1:20 CAP Apr 2026 release

10:08 The refreshed SAP Architecture Center

11:13 CAP AI Plugin for Node.js

11:57 DYK #14 CW18

Transcription

00;00;00;17 - 00;00;06;15
Announcer Guy
This is the SAP Developer News for April 30th, 2026.

00;00;06;18 - 00;00;09;26
DJ Adams
This is an important announcement relating to a security

00;00;09;27 - 00;00;45;02
DJ Adams
incident concerning some SAP packages on NPM, specifically MBT, the Multi Target Application Build tool and some of the database related modules in the cap.js scope. SQLite, Postgres and DB service. The incident a software supply chain attack in the context of the Shai Hulud campaign, occurred yesterday and has the full attention of teams inside of SAP who were engaged within a couple of hours of it happening.

00;00;45;04 - 00;01;21;06
DJ Adams
For more details on the affected package versions, how it happened, how to find out if you're affected and what you should do right now, you should head over to this Step security post, which has an excellent analysis and detailed action plan. We'll leave you with the details here of the specific package versions that have been compromised, and strongly recommend you take the appropriate actions described in the Posts Recovery Steps section.

00;01;21;08 - 00;01;53;25
Also DJ Adams
The latest CAP Release, April 2026 is out. This brings cap Node.js to 9.9, as well as the CDS-DK to 9.9, CAP Java to 4.9, and the CDS compiler to 6.9. Perhaps most significantly, though, is that this release looks forward to the upcoming next major release that CDS ten, in that some of the features and facilities introduced this time around are to help you prepare and move to CAP 10.

00;01;53;28 - 00;02;18;17
Also DJ Adams
Keeping your cap developments up to date is an important aspect in maintenance, and if you want to learn more, we've got a recent Expert Session episode for you on this very topic that you can bookmark and watch later. Okay, let's start looking at a few highlights of this current release. Starting with these details relating to help you plan for this move to CAP 10 later this year.

00;02;18;20 - 00;02;56;23
Also DJ Adams
First, Node.js support Node.js 22 becomes the minimum supported runtime after all will be at Node.js 26. As the lights version this coming October already, and in one of a number of consistent moves towards fewer external dependencies, the runtime native node SQLite library is now the default driver for SQLite. Next up in the area of help with release transitioning is an overhaul of feature and behavior.

00;02;56;23 - 00;03;36;08
Also DJ Adams
Flex. The CAP team have put in a lot of effort to put structure, transparency, and process around the changes that we need to pay attention to and act upon from release to release. The feature flags, each of which describe a change in behavior, have been organized and follow a lifecycle. When a flag is first introduced, the existing current or soon to be old behavior is the default value in the transition to the next major release, the new behavior becomes the default, and then in the release beyond that, the flag is removed.

00;03;36;10 - 00;04;15;06
Also DJ Adams
This means you can comfortably check in plenty of time and adjust as you need to before it's too late. Related to this is the convenient collection of flags in the try it out section, which you can add to your configuration to test your services and apps in CDS 9.9 but with CDS 10 behavior to see what breaks. Still, on the topic of looking ahead to cap ten, CAP Java will move to major version five supporting Spring Boot four, and there's a migration guide that you can use to prepare for that move.

00;04;15;08 - 00;04;44;10
Also DJ Adams
Last, but certainly not least, in this context of breaking changes, be aware that the CDS compiler now emits a hard error and has done actually since compiler version 6.5, where it encounters a security related annotation of something that doesn't exist. Now, moving beyond the topic of preparation for CDS ten, there still plenty to report in this April 2026 release.

00;04;44;10 - 00;05;11;17
Also DJ Adams
Let me pick out some highlights for you on the topic of vector embeddings. There's enhanced support for the Java on time and also in the CDS compiler, the CDL built in vector type, which in the past was only available for use with Hana, can now be used with H2 and SQLite for local development, and there's also beta support for Postgres.

00;05;11;19 - 00;05;50;02
Also DJ Adams
In addition, the Java runtime now also supports cosine similarity and L2 distance functions for similarity search and retrieval. Augmented generation with vector embeddings. Now, moreover, there's a new beta vector embedding function supported by the Java runtime that can work on text data directly in HANA. Now let's look at some CDS language updates in this release. Declarative constraints introduced at the end of last year are now generally available GA ready for production use.

00;05;50;04 - 00;06;25;20
Also DJ Adams
Note that there's a small change with this GA release regarding error codes. Now the CDS family of languages is becoming more powerful and expressive every month. In particular, when combined view definitions in CDL can now be extended using CQL clauses. Also, while we're familiar with using the Extend directive to add, say, elements and annotations to our definitions, we can now also use it to add enum values to derived enum types.

00;06;25;23 - 00;06;53;26
Also DJ Adams
There are updates in the Node.js runtime to many of them in a particular area, which I'll introduce by means of a quote from a computer scientist, programing language pioneer, and first recipient of the Alan Turing Award. Alan Perlis. Quote A software system can best be designed if the testing is interlaced with the designing, instead of being used after the design.

00;06;53;29 - 00;07;49;29
Also DJ Adams
So this release brings CDS test version 1.0, which will play a major role in the CAP product portfolio and related. In this release, we see an upgrade to version six of the latest Chai assertion library, added support for V test, which you should possibly consider as the primary choice for your testing framework these days, and following the same philosophy as we've heard about already, the dependency on the third party library, Axios is now replaced with Node.js native fetch API in this testing context, and the fetch API also means that we can simplify our development setups by relying on that also for remote communication scenarios instead of the SAP, which of course is still required for

00;07;49;29 - 00;08;18;08
Also DJ Adams
hybrid and production scenarios due to, for example, the important use of BTP destinations. There are also some changes and updates in the Java runtime, most notably regarding how services are served now. An application service will only serve by default. OData v2 and OData v4. There's a new property CDS protocols defaults that you can set for others instead in other dates.

00;08;18;08 - 00;08;49;27
Also DJ Adams
For other updates for the Java runtime, there is now support for CQL date and time functions to extract info from a timestamp and calculate durations, there are improvements on how you can specify and control protocol and path configurations. There's new documentation for adopting brownfield projects in the context of Spring Boot, and there are efficiency improvements in how draft deletion is done.

00;08;49;29 - 00;09;20;05
Also DJ Adams
So last but certainly not least, here are some highlights from updates in this release for the cap plugins area. Version 2.0 of the Change Tracking plugin bring significant performance benefits. Offers a new hierarchical view of changes using a tree table, and as support for CXL in the changelog annotation. There's also a migration plan available for moving to this new version.

00;09;20;08 - 00;10;01;23
Also DJ Adams
There are lots of updates and improvements in the area of attachments in both the Java and Node.js runtime context. And finally, there are two new plugins a print service plugin which, as you can probably guess, integrates with SAP Print Service and a process plugin which offers facilities for interaction with SAP build process automation. There's quite a release. Make sure you head on over to the detailed release page in Capire paying particular attention to the support and facilities that I've mentioned made available for the upcoming major release in June.

00;10;01;26 - 00;10;07;13
Also DJ Adams
Link to that copy page is in the description.

00;10;07;15 - 00;10;31;24
Antonio Maradiaga
SAP developers. The SAP Architecture Center just got a big refresh and it's worth checking it out. The website has been redesigned, search and navigation have been improved, but honestly, the most exciting part is in the new look. But the content. There are new practical architectures covering agents, AI and AI agents on SAP, BTP using Joule Studio, and the SAP cloud SDK for AI.

00;10;31;27 - 00;10;57;17
Antonio Maradiaga
There's also a whole section on integrated and extending duel across your landscape with SAP for HANA and SuccessFactors. Also, remember that the Architecture Center is a community driven, open source project. We can all contribute to it. The team has streamline the process with better docs, clear guidelines, and a VS Code integration to make it easier for you to submit your own reference architectures.

00;10;57;17 - 00;11;07;28
Antonio Maradiaga
So if you build something cool, please share it. Check out piers announcement on the SAP Architecture Center link in the description.

00;11;08;00 - 00;11;13;27
Antonio Maradiaga
Asta luego.

00;11;14;00 - 00;11;15;01
Kevin Riedelsheimer
Hey, everybody.

00;11;15;03 - 00;11;45;18
Kevin Riedelsheimer
Today's news item comes to you straight from the beautiful city of Budapest. The CAP team just released a brand new plugin for cap-js which is the AI plugin, and it allows you to easily to connect to SAP AI Core and handle APIs from SAP AI Core straight from your CAP service. And as well, it gives you out of the support for field recommendations on your UI using SAP Rapid-One model.

00;11;45;19 - 00;11;58;29
Kevin Riedelsheimer
So if you're interested in checking out this brand new plugin, head over to GitHub and read everything about it. Try it out right now. Today.

00;11;59;02 - 00;12;21;21
Shrini Neelamegam
Did you know you can define logic in the global canvas and trigger it from anywhere in your app? Global canvas is ideal for managing app wide events, shape changes, and reusable flows. It has no user interface and the logic defined in the global canvas can be triggered from anywhere in the app, making it a powerful tool for centralizing shared functionality.

00;12;21;24 - 00;12;45;06
Shrini Neelamegam
If your app has repeating logic, for example, enabling an X button after user selection, you can define it once in the global canvas and trigger it across pages to avoid duplication. You can define a receive event in the global canvas to handle the state changes, and then trigger that event from buttons on the individual pages, eliminating the need to duplicate the same event.

00;12;45;06 - 00;12;47;11
Shrini Neelamegam
Handling logic across multiple pages.

entry.title: Re: SuccessFactors running with ADP payroll engine
entry.content: I'm not sure how much help I can be, but we do use ADP for payroll. We use SuccessFactors as a system of record for HR info and extract demo data (changes, new hires, terms) every day and manually import it into ADP using the data bridge. We don't do it on the weekends since it is a manual process, and we load the weekend files on Monday. We use UKG for timekeeping data. We olny import what's needed into ADP from SF. The info from SF would be things like hire dates, rate of pay, address, phone number, ssn, pay group, and tax info.

entry.title: Re: Useful links to align SAP SuccessFactors with the EU Pay Transparency Directive
entry.content:
And here is an additional expert opinion on this topic: "I think position VS posting location is easy, as legally, customer only has to disclose the pay for the location of the position (the seat). Where it becomes more complex is telework locations. We addressed that for the US locations only. Customer wanted all geozones to be displayed for the telework jobs. We built fields to capture 4 geo zones from position and mapped all 4 ranges onto the posting. we also had to get an approval from the customer's legal team. but i wonder if we can apply a similar logic in this case as well... thinking about this more, I wonder if we can have some kind of lookup rule created that can null out all geo zones but those that are relevant to the location(s). this is definitely not a straight forward config and will require defined customer requirements and a creative 'outside-the-box' solution."

 

Maybe engage with SAP Services through your CSM or with an implementation partner to run mockups of these designs.

Thanks

Xavier

entry.title: Re: S/4HANA Public Cloud: How to copy custom extension fi...
entry.content:
No, SAP does not such a BAdI to directly modify standard fields (like Product/Material MATNR) on a sales order item.
Public Cloud extensibility only supports updating custom extension fields (using SD_SLS_MODIFY_ITEM), not overriding clean core standard data.

Regards, Pranay Sahay

entry.title: Re: Where to create new FS item : Dev with transport to Q...
entry.content:
Based on SAP best practices, new FS items should be created in DEV and transported to QA and PRD. Creating FS items directly in PRD is discouraged as it leads to system divergence and causes issues during upgrades.

All configurations relating to MM or EWM like movement types or field selections should be created in DEV and transported to QA & PRD.

GR related transactions like GR/IR, MIRO, MIGO will occur in PRD as monthly transactions.

Regards, Pranay Sahay

entry.title: Re: Journal entry type not editable in the Reprocess Bank...
entry.content:
The Reprocess Bank Statement Items (F1520) app, the journal entry type is typically non-editable if the item has already been partially processed, locked by another user, or if the system has defaulted a specific posting type that cannot be switched (e.g., changing from Customer to Supplier).

Here are the actions you can take to change the journal entry type:
1. Reverse the Posted Item
If the bank statement item has already been posted with the wrong type, you must reverse it to make changes and then Reprocess.

2. Or Check for Locks and Status

3. Or Use "Reset" to Revert to Initial State

4. Or Create a Manual Reprocessing Rule

Regards, Pranay Sahay

entry.title: Re: Week 4: AI Developer Challenge SAP-RPT-1 Model
Re: Unable to Assign Collection Management Attributes via...
entry.content:
I am not certain but please try this as recommended solution:

To assign Collections Management data (Segment, Group, Strategy, Specialist) in S/4HANA Public Cloud, you cannot use the standard API_BUSINESS_PARTNER.

  • Instead, you must use the Collections Management - Manage OData service (API_UDM_COLLECTIONS_MANAGEMENT) or the Business Partner - Create, Update (via PATCH) specifically targeting the A_BPCollectionSegment entity set within the existing service, ensuring the UDM000 role exists first.

Recommended Approaches for Automation

  • 1. Use the Dedicated Collections API:
    Service Name: API_UDM_COLLECTIONS_MANAGEMENT
    Entity: A_BPCollectionSegment
    Usage: Use this API immediately following the business partner creation/update to PATCH the collection segment, strategy, group, and specialist.
  • 2. Alternative: BTP Custom Action Handler:
    If using SAP BTP Integration Suite, design a custom logic that first calls the API_BUSINESS_PARTNER for role UDM000 and, upon successful confirmation, makes a second call to the A_BPCollectionSegment entity within the Business Partner API or API_UDM_COLLECTIONS_MANAGEMENT.
  • 3. Resolving Job Scheduling Constraints: Instead of using "Created On" in the standard jobs, use the Collection Profile Assignment functionality within the Manage Customer Master Data app, or automate the "Change Segment Data" job to run daily using a Relative Time Filter (if available) or by scheduling it via the Application Jobs app with dynamic parameters to handle new customers.

 

  • Key Data to Include in API Payloads (A_BPCollectionSegment)
    BusinessPartner (BP Number)
    CollectionSegment (Required)
    CollectionGroup
    CollectionStrategy
    CollectionSpecialist
  • By utilizing the dedicated collections API directly, you bypass the limitations of the general Business Partner service.

Regards, Pranay Sahay

entry.title: Re: Event Based Costing - WIP Not Decreasing on Partial G...
entry.content:
Hello,

In SAP Event-Based Costing, partial goods receipts (GR) for materials with price control 'V' (Moving Average) and no standard cost often result in zero-value inventory postings, leaving costs in WIP. Because the system calculates inventory value based on the existing moving average price at the time of GR, and with no preliminary cost estimate, the GR is valued at $0

Here are the specific actions to resolve this:

Implement Preliminary Costing: Ensure the production order has a calculated estimated cost (preliminary cost estimate) before doing a partial GR. This allows the system to value the partial GR correctly.

Use Standard Price (Recommended): For consistent inventory valuation in manufacturing, consider using Price Control 'S' (Standard Price) with a released cost estimate. This ensures GR is always valued at standard, reducing issues with partial receipts.

WIP Clearing Strategy: With Event-Based Costing, WIP should decrease as GR occurs. If this is not happening, confirm the order is not locked and that the components are fully consumed and technical completion (TECO) is not yet set.

Validate Valuation Variants: Check that the valuation variant for the order (defined in OPL1/OPN1) allows for accurate cost component calculations during the partial GR process.

Component Consumption: Verify that all component consumptions are properly posted (261 movement) before the GR, as the value of the partial GR is calculated based on the cost of the components.

Regards, Pranay Sahay

entry.title: Re: Demo Scenario: Clean Core Extensibility with ABAP Cloud and SAP Build
entry.content:
@Mani_P_S , I have many thoughts on this but will try to be brief to avoid spiraling into a long rant. 😅 Although this probably deserves a longer article.

1. "Clean core" is not the goal by itself. What would SAP customers achieve by implementing such architecture change? "Clean core"? That's not going to impress anyone. What business value would this add exactly? (This part is very frequently missing from the SAP posts in general, I have to say.)

2. The "after" diagram doesn't really look appealing because it looks more complex and solutions like BPA would potentially come at additional cost. The "before" solution would have almost 0 cost to the customers, after the initial implementation. And see p.1 the advantage (other than "look, ma, clean core!") isn't clear. 

3. It's quite possible there is additional advantage by moving the Loyalty program maintenance off the core S/4HANA system. And it might even be better to use CAP for this and not ABAP environment. But one would need way more information to make a decision here. 

4. Scale is of huge importance in these solutions. Volume of orders, number of times the "tier upgrade" might be required would need to be considered. In simplest case, I'd even skip workflow altogether. And this is where the "after" diagram quickly becomes an overkill. I get it that this is just a random idea but I feel this should be at least mentioned in the post. Or even better, maybe look at variations: a "poor man's" solution vs. more robust/scalable one. 

5. Obviously I can't speak for everyone, but in all 20 years of ABAP experience I've never created a new module pool or Web Dynpro app. I doubt anyone would go with a WD app for this, unless they absolutely would have to. This would much more likely be a couple of Z-tables with SM30-based tcode or a simple ABAP report. Not a huge deal but I feel like the post is trying to make the "before" part look worse intentionally.

By the way, there is a very cool feature in ECC called Logistics Info System (LIS) that I would totally rig here for tracking sales to determine loyalty level. It is configurable and it's a standard functionality to collect/summarize sales data whenever a sales order is placed. So, the loyalty part might actually not even need any Z table. I'd store the loyalty tier in a customer master field and run a report monthly to update the value based on the sales volume. And have either custom tcode or custom authorization to override it. Bonus: customer master field can then be used in the discount configuration. ECC is awesome. 😀 But I digress.

6. Again, I get that it's "a concept of a demo" but that's kind of a problem. Using a shallow idea with many details missing doesn't allow to demonstrate pretty much anything clearly. I'm thinking a more realistic scenario (which you could probably get by talking to actual customers) would be way more helpful. This example "yada-yadaed over the best part", I feel. 

Side note: SAP Community website was practically destroyed in latest migration, and the biggest advantage the community team stated was to have multiple authors for a blog post. And here we are, not even using it. Oh irony.

entry.title: Re: Leased Assets yearly rent payment adjustment
entry.content:
In SAP, lease rental agreements are managed through the Flexible Real Estate Management (RE-FX) that is designed to comply with IFRS 16/ASC 842. By creating a lease contract (T-code RECN) with payment conditions defined (e.g., annual frequency), SAP automates the generation of yearly payment cash flows, including interest, principal, and Right-of-Use (ROU) asset depreciation.

But I believe you need a simple solution then create a vendor contract in SAP-MM using ME31k and take help of some MM consultant to configure.
Logistics > Materials Management > Purchasing > Outline Agreement > Contract > ME31K

Regards, Pranay Sahay

entry.title: Re: Can reporting FS item REPT be used in the standard FS...
entry.content:
In SAP S/4HANA Group Reporting, using FS items of type REPT (reporting items) is the standard method for calculated items, enabling flexible formulas within reports without posting actual data, thus avoiding the need to transfer standard INC/LEQ/AST items to a new hierarchy. Creating calculated items as posted items (INC/LEQ/AST) via rules is generally not recommended, as it complicates data transparency and auditing.

Clarification of your points:

01. Hierarchy Approach: No. You do not need to move all standard INC, LEQ, and AST items to a new hierarchy when using REPT items. REPT items act as "virtual" line items in reports, allowing you to create formulas like [Calculated Item] = [Item A] - [Item B] directly within a query or FS item formula setting. The standard FS items remain in your standard hierarchies.

02. Posted Item Approach: Not Recommended. Creating calculated items as posted types (INC, LEQ, AST) populated via substitution/validation rules is considered bad practice. It violates the principle of storing only raw data in the ACDOCU table and creates "hidden" formula-driven data that is hard to trace, audit, and reconcile compared to REPT items which are calculated on the fly.

Best Practice: Create new FS items specifically for your calculation, set their type to REPT, and define the calculation logic in the "Calculated Formula" field within the FS item maintenance. This ensures a clean separation between posted source data and calculated reporting data.

Regards, Pranay Sahay

entry.title: Re: Advanced FCV unrealized posting remains open after pa...
entry.content:
In SAP S/4HANA Advanced Foreign Currency Valuation, the four G/L accounts are used to manage valuation differences. Unrealized Gain-Loss Accounts record the revenue/expense from exchange rate changes, while Reset Accounts automatically reverse these valuation postings in the following period to ensure accurate net open item valuation. 
  • Unrealized Gain Account: Credited with exchange rate gains that are not yet realized.
  • Unrealized Loss Account: Debited with exchange rate losses that are not yet realized.
  • Reset Account for Unrealized Gain: Used to post the reversing entry for the gain, typically a technical balance sheet account.
  • Reset Account for Unrealized Loss: Used to post the reversing entry for the loss, also a technical balance sheet account. 
This structure allows for precise management of valuation postings, ensuring gains and losses are only recognized in P&L when realized.

entry.title: Re: Advanced FCV unrealized posting remains open after pa...

No, the Advanced Foreign Currency Valuation configuration does not fully replace transaction key KDF. You must still maintain accounts in both locations because they serve two entirely different business events in SAP S/4HANA.

Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: How does SAP on server landscape file caching work?
entry.content:
The information about the on server landscape file caching can be found on the SAP Frontend Installation Guide:

2.3 Caching Remote Configuration Files
Configuration files (SAPUILandscape.xml files) loaded from a remote server will be cached by default. The cache folder is %APPDATA%\SAP\LogonServerConfigCache.
This feature can be switched on and off via the check box Allow caching of remote configuration files in the SAP Logon Options Configuration Files dialog. By default, it is activated. In the same dialog, you find information on the the XML file and the caching path.
There are two options available to define with which interval SAP Logon checks the server for new configuration files:

  • Update cache on every SAP Logon (Pad) start
  • Update cache only in an interval of [hours]

When using the first option, SAP Logon (Pad) checks, if the file on the server is newer than the one in the cache; if so, SAP Logon (Pad) exchanges the file. With the second option the server is only contacted, if the last enquiry or download took place more than x hours ago. This saves long roundtrips in slow networks.

 Note
Using the second option, the check also only takes place at SAP Logon start, not when a SAP GUI
connection to an SAP system is opened.

In case the corresponding server cannot be reached and the server configuration file is not available anymore, the cached files will be used at SAP Logon restart and displayed in the SAP Logon Options Configuration Files dialog under Loaded Configuration Files.

In case that the given server configuration file and the cache files are not available, only local configuration files will be used and SAP Logon displays a warning when started

entry.title: Re: how to create financial transaction type
entry.content:
In the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, you generally cannot create new financial transaction types (e.g., in Treasury or Asset Accounting) from scratch, as this is restricted to maintain upgrade stability. Instead, you must use standard, pre-delivered transaction types and configure them to meet business requirements via Self-Service Configuration UIs (SSCUI) in the SAP Central Business Configuration (CBC) tool.

Steps to Configure Financial Transaction Types

If you are working with Treasury and Risk Management, follow these steps to use and configure existing types:
Access Configuration: Go to the SAP Central Business Configuration (CBC) or use the Fiori app Manage Your Solution.

Navigate to Transaction Settings: Locate the area for Treasury and Risk Management > Financial Transactions > Configure Transaction Type Parameters (SSCUI 103014).

Use Existing Types: Review the predefined transaction types delivered by SAP (e.g., 100-Investment, 200-Borrowing).

Adjust Parameters: Select an existing transaction type that closely matches your needs and adjust the settings:
Company Code Assignment: Define which company codes can use the type.
Partner Roles: Define allowable counterparties,
Field Selection: Configure which fields are required or optional.

Save and Deploy: Save your changes and deploy the configuration to the productive system.

Regards, Pranay Sahay

entry.content:
The team can read or review some of the online tutorials like:

  1. SAP Datasphere Overview:  https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=t6KoDcATK-M 


  2. Exploring SAP Datasphere: https://learning.sap.com/courses/exploring-sap-datasphere?searchId=5ba74d72-1777-49e9-bbc0-8343fad6f022&listPosition=5 
  3. SAP Datasphere with SAP BW Bridge: https://learning.sap.com/courses/sap-datasphere-sap-bw-bridge?searchId=d73dcbf1-d4af-4d95-a155-52c8211246bd&listPosition=6 
  4. Exploring SAP BTP - Nuts and bolts of BTP -
    1. Part 1:https://learning.sap.com/courses/operating-sap-business-technology-platform?searchId=43f829ba-d86f-473c-96fb-607c689eba96&listPosition=1 
    2. Part 2:  https://learning.sap.com/courses/exploring-sap-business-technology-platform?searchId=3735490a-0700-4b02-aed8-4c3e89cd9825&listPosition=2 
  5. End to End SAP Build: https://learning.sap.com/courses/experiencing-end-to-end-sap-build?searchId=6e610bd5-953f-4194-814e-5fea464ec2e1&listPosition=17 

And in a nutshell (After taking those classes above), you will be the expert you needed and the below steps will be easy for you. 

1. You need to expose your data in Datasphere and mark your view/perspective as "Exposed for Consumption".
2. You need to create a Database user or an Oauth Client in Datasphere to generate an API credentials.
3. In your BTP cockpit, create a destination pointing to the Datasphere API URL.
4. In the SAP Build "Data" tab, select the "SAP System" and pick your Datasphere Destination.

Regards,
Mark

entry.title: Adapting SAP Joule for Purchase Requisition Creation in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
entry.content:
Hello, and thank you for taking the time to read this blog post!

As a member of the SAP S/4HANA RIG (Regional Implementation Group), our mission is to share collective insights and hands‑on experience with the broader SAP community through blog posts and SAP Learning courses. To date, we have published more than 700 blog posts and delivered numerous learning offerings to help customers successfully navigate SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition.

Today, I would like to show how you can leverage SAP Joule in SAP S/4HANA and, more importantly, how you can adapt it to your specific business needs. While this example focuses on Purchase Requisition (PR) creation, the same principles can be applied to many other business processes.

SAP Joule and Purchase Requisition Creation

As you can see in the video below, as of May 2026, Joule can guide an end user through the creation of a Purchase Requisition. Additional details are available in the SAP Help Portal here: Create Purchase Requisitions.

 

This capability is already very useful for many customers. However, we also know that every customer has unique requirements. After presenting this process, I frequently received the following questions:

  • Can Joule create a Purchase Requisition using our own document type?
  • Can Joule populate additional standard fields, such as the PR Description?
  • I created a custom field in the F1481 - Custom fields app and enabled it in the respective Purchase requisition API. Could that custom field be included in the prompt?
  • Our team only creates PRs for materials. Can Joule assume I will request a material and skip the initial question?
  • Is it possible to request a PR that includes all the required information in a single prompt?
  • Can multiple materials be included directly during PR creation, rather than as a follow‑up step?

The short answer to all of these questions is yes—by leveraging Joule Studio through Agents and Skills.

As part of the SAP Build portfolio, Joule Studio allows you to tailor Joule to your specific business requirements using an intuitive, user-friendly interface. It enables you to extend standard capabilities, connect to existing SAP and customer-specific services, and guide how Joule responds based on business context and input.

In addition, Joule Studio lets you define AI agents with clear roles and areas of focus, ensuring Joule behaves in a way that aligns with your processes and supports users more effectively in their daily work.

And rather than just taking my word for it, you can see it in action in the following video:

For this case, I used a single Prompt:

“Using Easy Purchaser, create a PR 'ZNB', "Blog Joule", with: TG11, plant: 1710, 5 PC, date: 01/06/2026; TG12, plant: 1710, 7 PC, date: 01/06/2026, ZJoule: Demo”.

Compared with the initial video, Joule did not ask for a source of supply or whether the request was for a material or a service. It also allowed me to select the document type “ZNB”, populate the PR description, and fill the custom field “ZJoule.” This behaviour is intentional and reflects how Joule was configured in Joule Studio to align with the business process and reduce unnecessary user input.

 

Key Takeaway

SAP delivers a standard experience for Joule‑driven object creation. At the same time, you remain in control. By leveraging SAP Joule Studio, you can tailor Joule to meet your organization’s unique needs—without being limited to a fixed, predefined flow.

 

The SAP S/4HANA RIG Team is preparing a series of technical and functional blogs to walk you through some examples. The next blog will focus on the technical steps to enable this scenario and to adapt the Joule Agent to your business needs. Links will be updated here. Stay tuned!

Thank you again for reading, and I encourage you to embrace the latest innovations that come with SAP S/4HANA. If you found this blog helpful, please consider giving it a Like or sharing it with others.

I would also love to hear your thoughts and feedback—feel free to leave a comment. If you have any questions, please post them in the Q&A section using the tag SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. We will do our best to respond as quickly as possible.

 

Thanks,
Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA RIG Team

entry.title: Re: Week 4: AI Developer Challenge SAP-RPT-1 Model
How AI Helped Me Understand and Decompress SAP Content Server 7.5 CSCompress PDF Files
entry.content:
Building a Standalone CSCompress Decompressor for SAP Content Server with AI

When I first encountered a CSCompress file from SAP Content Server, I assumed there had to be a simple way to open it. There was not. This article tells the story of how AI helped me analyze the format, validate it against real compressed and original files, and create a standalone solution for decompressing SAP Content Server PDF files.

 

 

Figure 1. Shows HTML page that is used as standalone tool to decompress PDF files compressed with CSCompress (SAP Contet Server 7.5)

 

I already knew from the start what the real problem was: I had a PDF file stored in compressed form, and I wanted to get the original readable PDF back outside SAP.

Like many of us would do, I first tried the quick workaround. I renamed the file to .zip and attempted to open it as a normal archive. That did not work. The file remained just as unreadable as before, and it was clear that this was not a standard ZIP-based compression scenario.

So I turned to AI and asked it to help me analyze the compression format and find a way to decompress the file properly.

At that point, the challenge was perfectly clear:

take a CSCompress file from SAP Content Server, decompress it, and recover the original PDF.

And yes — I accepted the challenge. 😛

 

First Attempt: Analyze the Compressed File Directly

The first step was to inspect the compressed file itself and see whether the format could be recognized immediately.

It was clear quite quickly that the file was not a normal PDF anymore:

it did not start with a %PDF- header,
it could not be opened by standard archive utilities,
and the payload clearly had some SAP-specific wrapping.

AI helped me inspect the binary structure and reason about what the first bytes might represent, but one compressed file alone was not enough to fully understand the format with confidence.

At that point, the investigation needed a better strategy.

Switching to a Controlled Test Approach

Instead of trying to reverse-engineer everything from a single mysterious blob, I changed the method.

I asked AI to generate several known PDF files for testing. I then uploaded those files into SAP Content Server, allowed the system to compress them, and collected both sides of the puzzle:

the original PDF files
the matching CSCompress-compressed files

This gave me several original/compressed file pairs, which made the analysis much more reliable.

That was the turning point.

Once I had real pairs, I was no longer guessing in the dark. AI could now compare the files directly, validate assumptions, and check whether a decompression attempt really reconstructed the original file byte-for-byte.

AI was Looking for Technical Clues in Open Source SAP-Related Code

After comparing the sample pairs, AI started looking for technical clues in open-source SAP-related compression code.

This search eventually led to useful source files such as:

vpa105CsObjInt.cpp
hpa107cslzh.h

These files did not provide a ready-made standalone tool that AI could simply copy and run. But they did provide something just as valuable: structure and direction.

From that code, AI could confirm the structure of the CSCompress wrapper header and extract important details such as:

where the original length is stored,
how the algorithm is encoded,
and what fixed marker bytes appear in the compressed file.

At that point, the work for AI became a combination of:

open-source code reading,
binary comparison of real files,
and AI-assisted reasoning and validation.
Understanding the Header

The validated sample files used in my tests all followed the same general structure.

The compressed document begins with an 8-byte CSCompress header:

bytes 0..3: original uncompressed file length, stored as a little-endian 32-bit integer
byte 4: combined version/algorithm field
bytes 5..6: magic bytes 1F 9D
byte 7: a special field used in this compression variant

For example, a header like this:

F4 06 00 00 12 1F 9D 02

can be interpreted as:

original file size = 0x000006F4
version = 1
algorithm = 2
magic = 1F 9D
special = 2

That was a major milestone, because it meant the file was no longer an opaque mystery. It had a recognizable wrapper and clearly structured metadata.

What the Sample Pairs Proved

The original/compressed file pairs turned out to be essential.

They allowed AI to confirm that:

the first four bytes really represented the original file length,
the test files consistently used algorithm 2,
the same structure repeated across multiple examples,
and, most importantly, the decompressed output could be verified against the original file content exactly.

This is worth emphasizing:

without the open-source SAP-related code, AI would not have understood the header and format direction so quickly;

without the real original/compressed sample pairs, AI would not have been able to prove that the standalone decompression logic actually worked on the real SAP Content Server output.

Both parts were necessary.

From Analysis to Implementation

Once the format was understood well enough, the next step was implementation.

The first working version was written in Python, because Python is ideal for binary parsing, rapid experimentation, and repeated validation.

That version was very useful for proving that the decompression logic worked. It allowed AI to:

parse the header,
isolate the payload,
apply the required bit-level handling,
decompress the content,
and compare the output with the original PDF.

After that, I explored additional standalone implementations in other languages and formats.

The decompressor was then implemented in:

Python
PHP
HTML/JavaScript

I also experimented with VBScript-based Windows usability, mainly to make the tool easier to use in desktop environments. In practice, the most convenient result turned out to be the HTML/JavaScript standalone version, because it:

runs by simple double-click,
requires no SAP system,
requires no external libraries,
and works comfortably on Windows through the browser.

That version later evolved further and was enhanced to calculate:

SHA-256
SHA-1
MD5

for both the input and output files, making validation easier and more transparent.

What AI Actually Contributed

I want to be precise here, because that matters.

AI simply hand me a perfect ready-made decompression function on the first try.

What AI did help with was:

analyzing the structure of the compressed files,
identifying patterns in the headers,
reasoning about what certain bytes might represent,
looking in open-source SAP-related code,
and turning those clues into practical standalone tools.

In other words, AI was used as a Senior Developer.
AI model used here was: GPT-5.4 Thinking

The final solution came from combining:

AI-assisted analysis,
AI open-source code reading,
real SAP Content Server sample pairs,
and repeated testing until the output matched the original files exactly.


Current Scope of the Solution

The current implementation is validated using PDF file pairs, because that was the real-life use case I was working on.

Technically, the decompression logic operates on the CSCompress container format, not on PDF syntax alone. That means the same logic may also work for other file types compressed with the same CSCompress variant.

However, the current repository and validation examples are centered on PDF-based test cases, and I think it is important to state that clearly.

Everything Is Published on GitHub

To make the work reproducible and useful for others, I published the project on GitHub.

The repository includes:

the standalone decompressor implementations
the validation examples
and the supporting material used during the investigation

GitHub repository:

CSCompress_Decompressor

Conclusion

What started as a practical need — “I know this is a compressed PDF, I just want the original file back” — turned into a very enjoyable technical investigation.

The interesting part was not only finding a way to decompress the file, but understanding enough of the format to build standalone tools that work outside SAP.

In the end, the solution was not the result of one magic trick. It was the result of:

trying the obvious shortcut,
failing,
building controlled test samples,
AI reading the right technical clues, 
AI validating against real file pairs,
and iterating until the output was correct.

That is also what made the process fun.

If you have worked with SAP Content Server internals, ArchiveLink, CSCompress files, or similar reverse-engineering tasks, feel free to share your thoughts, comments, or improvements. I would be very interested to hear how others approached similar problems.

entry.title: Unused Coding Block in SAP – Impact on Upgrade?
entry.content:
Scenario
We implemented a custom coding block in our development system. After design changes, this coding block is no longer required.

Current Situation
SAP has confirmed that field suppression is technically possible, but they indicated that it may create issues during a future upgrade. Their recommendation is to restore the system to a state before the coding block was implemented.

Constraint
A rollback is not practical in our case because the system has been actively used for about a month after the coding block implementation, and multiple transports have already been created. Restoring the system and re-importing transports would likely create conflicts with open objects and require significant effort.

Proposed Approach
Instead of performing a rollback, we are considering suppressing the field, renaming it to indicate it is unused, and ensuring it is not consumed anywhere in reports, forms, Fiori applications, or interfaces. The coding block would remain technically present in the extended tables but would not be used functionally.

Questions
1. What is the actual upgrade impact if an unused coding block remains?
2. Is suppression sufficient, or will this cause issues in SPAU/SPDD or upgrades?
3. Has anyone upgraded a system with inactive/unused coding blocks?

Looking for practical inputs before taking a decision.

#SAP #S4HANA #SAPUpgrade #SAPDevelopment #SAPFICO #CodingBlock #SAPCustomization #SPAU #SPDD #SAPTransport #SAPArchitecture #SAPCommunity

entry.title: 🇫🇷 Hands-on with CAP CDS
entry.content:
This CodeJam gives you a thorough grounding in CAP CDS modelling.

In this hands-on workshop you'll become familiar and comfortable with the key features of CDS modelling, primarily focusing on the Conceptual Definition Language (CDL), the predominantly declarative domain-specific language (DSL) in the CDS family. You'll explore the different concepts that make CAP such a compelling framework for today's world of AI, where concision, correctness and the imperative to write less code is at the forefront of everyone's mind.

No prior experience is required, and this workshop is suitable for non-developers as well as developers. All you need is curiosity and a desire to learn!

This is an in-person event only (not virtual) and is on Thu 24 Sep 2026 in Paris, FR, kindly hosted by VINCI Energies Systèmes d'Information, thank you very much! Note that it takes place on the day before the planned SAP Inside Track Paris event on Fri 25 Sep 2026. 

⚠️Note that capacity for this CodeJam is 30 people, so please ensure you use the RSVP links on this page accordingly.

The instructor is Developer Advocate DJ Adams. 

There are essential prerequisites that must be completed by every attendee, before arriving at the event location.

For more information on SAP CodeJam events, to ask questions, and to chat with your fellow attendees, head over to the SAP CodeJam group.

entry.title: Re: SES rejected in Ariba -is the supplier notified?
entry.content:
See SAP Note 3526476/ KB0902525 -- "When parameter EnableSendingPaperBasedCopySES is enabled, B&I sends only the copy of SES to AN only when the SES is fully Approved and Processed in B&I. This SES copy in AN reaches with Approved status already."

A Buyer initiated SES that is rejected is internal only, isn't copied to SBN, and does not notify the supplier.

How can I make the PIR of a component product like a semi-finished product be consumed when it's a FERT product?

entry.title: Re: CK40N Cost Estimate Silently Truncating / Excluding V...
entry.content:
Instead of using base quantity = 1, scale the process material base quantity (e.g., 1,000 or 10,000). This reduces the per-unit cost estimate, keeping values within SAP’s supported range.

Break down BOMs into smaller logical units or run costing for sub-periods. Aggregate results outside CK40N to avoid exceeding the threshold. Meanwhile, raise an OSS incident with SAP for a long-term fix

entry.title: Re: WOCR Configuration to Create Separate Warehouse Order...
entry.content:
Hi,

Please check the consolidation group assigned to both the ODO's. Also check if the flag for 'Pack consolidation' is active at the ODO line item. Possibly it must be active. 

To achieve what you need, following must be done

  1. Each ODO to have a unique consolidation group.
  2. The flag for 'pack consolidation' must be unticked.
  3. Define WOCR limit filter which has a setup for 'Max  CGrp per WO' as 1. Standard limit filter CONS can be used
  4. Make sure the WOCR defined has the limit filter defined in #3. 
  5. Test out the setup.

P.S. The pack consolidation is maintained in the customer master record.

Hope this helps. 

Regards,

Sushant

entry.title: Re: Logging table SAPWSD./1CADMC/00000262 in source syste...
entry.content:
Hi,

For partitioning the table please check following document:

  • 2044468 - FAQ: SAP HANA Partitioning

    TableTypeColumns
    /1CADMC/<id>HASH

    IUUC_SEQUENCE

Further you can see useful information here:

  • 2000002 - FAQ: SAP HANA SQL Optimization

    Logging table selection

    When content in logging tables is replicated to the target systems, selections like the below example are executed:

    SELECT TOP 5000 *
    FROM "/1CADMC/00296603"
    WHERE "DOCNUM" >= ? 
      AND "DOCNUM" < ? 
      AND "IUUC_PROCESSED" IN (?, ?)
    ORDER BY "IUUC_PROCESSED", "DOCNUM", "MANDT", "COUNTER", "SEGNUM"

    In order to optimize these requests, you can consider the following aspects:

    • Keep the amount of data in logging tables as small as possible. Logging tables with an increased record count are reported by check ID M2540 ("SLT logging tables with significant record count") of the SAP HANA Mini Checks (SAP Note 1999993). See SAP Note 2882481 about tackling a high number of logged records, e.g. by reloading the table or defining ranges.

    • If the amount of data that has to be sorted is quite large and the package size is rather small ("TOP 5000" in the case above), you can maintain a larger value (e.g. 99999) in transaction LTRS -> "Replication Options" -> "Portion Size (Records)" or column NUM_RECS_LOGTAB of table IUUC_PERF_OPTION for the involved source table and regenerate the runtime object. Then you can process significantly more records and still sort only once. Be aware that the length of the field is limited to 5 characters, so values beyond 99999 aren't possible. When increasing the package size you also have to make sure that the data volume processed in one package (i.e. package size * length of source table record) doesn't exceed 1 GB.

    • In transaction LTRS you can use the "Performance Options" -> "Replication Options" to configure a parallelization of the replication. For this purpose, in section "Ranges for Logging Table" you can choose "Use Key Fields to calculate Ranges".

    • The above example uses an older, no longer recommended option to parallelize the replication by defining a parallelization column (DOCNUM in the above example) in PARALLEL_FIELDNAME of table IUUC_PERF_OPTION. This parallelization column is included as second column of an index after column IUUC_PROCESSED. As SAP HANA can't evaluate ranges in multi column indexes, you can manually create an additional single column index on the parallelization column (in this case on DOCNUM).

    See SAP Note 2882481 for more information.

If you have further specific questions regarding housekeeping activity, please open a case with proper component (CA-LT-SLT).

Regards,
Laszlo

entry.title: Re: [S/4HANA Cloud] Regarding the assembly and consumptio...
entry.content:
Why HALB worked but FERT does not is because

  • HALB (semi-finished) materials are normally planned with PIRs and consumed by dependent requirements from parent products.
  • FERT (finished) materials are usually planned with sales orders directly, and PIRs are consumed only if the planning strategy allows it.
  • When you switched the component from HALB to FERT, SAP started treating it as a finished good, so the PIR consumption logic changed.

Key settings that drive PIR consumption

1) Planning Strategy Group (Policy 70)
Strategy 70 is “Planning at assembly level.” This is designed for assemblies (HALB), not for finished goods. For FERT, you typically use strategies like 40 (Planning with final assembly) or 20 (Make-to-order), depending on whether you want PIRs consumed by sales orders.

2) Dual MRP Classification = 1
This means the material is treated as a planning material, but the classification must align with the strategy group.

3) Consumption Mode = 4
Backward/forward consumption is fine, but it only applies if the strategy group allows PIR consumption.

4) Forward/Reverse Consumption Period = 365
This is generous, but again, irrelevant if the strategy group does not trigger PIR consumption for FERT.

If you want to fix this, perform the below steps

  • Check the Planning Strategy Group for the FERT material in the material master (MRP 3 view). If you want PIRs to be consumed by sales orders, use strategy 40 (Planning with final assembly). Strategy 70 is not appropriate for FERT materials.
  • Verify the requirements class linked to the strategy group. It must allow PIR consumption. Run MD04 for the FERT material and check if the PIRs are being reduced when sales orders are entered. If not, the strategy group is the culprit.
  • If the FERT is really acting like a semi-finished component in your BOM, consider keeping it as HALB or using a strategy that mimics HALB behavior for PIR consumption.

entry.title: Re: Override Login Authentication Failure Handler
entry.content:
Dear Sumit,

The scenario involving an override of CustomLoginAuthenticationFailureHandler is not covered in the official SAP Framework Update documentation.

Based on the available documentation, the supported and documented areas include:

  • JWT customization using OAuth2TokenCustomizer or DefaultExtensibleJWTCustomizer beans
  • Configuration patterns for the resource server SecurityFilterChain
  • Use of the loginPageUri property in OAuthClientDetails for external login pages
  • An important architectural update: the password-based flow (/authorizationserver/login) has been deprecated and removed in the newer Spring Authorization Server. The recommended approach is to use Authorization Code flow with PKCE

As a result, the /authorizationserver/login form POST endpoint currently used by the Spartacus frontend represents a legacy implementation.

For Composable Storefront with Commerce 2211 (Framework Update), the officially supported approach is the Authorization Code + PKCE flow. This approach eliminates the AuthenticationFailureHandler redirect issue by design, as the authorization server does not issue HTTP 302 redirects directly to the SPA.

 

Kind Regards

Balaji

SAP Support

entry.title: Understanding SAP HANA Smart Data Access (SDA): Execution Boundaries and Remote Data Processing
entry.content:
Introduction
SAP HANA Smart Data Access (SDA) enables SAP HANA to access data stored in external systems without replicating the data into SAP HANA.

External systems are connected as remote sources, and their objects can be represented inside SAP HANA as virtual tables.
From an application perspective, these objects can be queried using standard SQL statements, while the data remains in the source system and is accessed during statement execution.

Official SAP HANA Smart Data Access documentation:
Data Access in the SAP HANA Cloud, SAP HANA Database

This article explains general processing behavior and supports correct interpretation during troubleshooting.
It does not provide configuration or performance tuning guidance.


Remote Sources and Virtual Tables
With SDA, SAP HANA integrates remote data access into SQL processing.
When a query references a virtual table, SAP HANA processes the statement, and data access is performed via the connected remote source during execution.
This behavior is part of the documented SAP HANA Smart Data Access architecture.


Query Processing Context
In SAP HANA Smart Data Access, SQL statements referencing virtual tables may involve both local SAP HANA processing and operations executed against the connected remote source.
As part of query processing, operations may be executed locally in SAP HANA or against the connected remote source.
Query execution may therefore include processing steps across system boundaries as part of normal system behavior.


Support-Relevant Distinction
From a support perspective, the following distinction is essential:
A statement can be initiated and processed by SAP HANA, while parts of the effective data processing may take place outside SAP HANA via the remote source and the communication layer.
Because of this execution boundary, the status of SAP HANA services alone does not describe the complete processing path when Smart Data Access is used.


Observations in Troubleshooting
During troubleshooting, it is possible that local SAP HANA processing appears normal while statements accessing virtual tables show different response times.

This can occur because the execution path may include:

  • the SAP HANA database
  • the network communication layer
  • the connected remote source

The overall response time therefore reflects the complete end-to-end processing path.


Documented SDA Scenarios
SAP documentation and SAP Notes describe different SDA-related scenarios that can influence behavior during execution.

SDA FAQ:
https://me.sap.com/notes/2180119

Examples include:

  • Remote source object discovery error scenario
    https://me.sap.com/notes/2572731
  • SDA remote source connection test / communication error scenario
    https://me.sap.com/notes/3470273
  • Remote source object access / closed connection scenario
    https://me.sap.com/notes/2557181

These scenarios may occur while the SAP HANA database itself remains available.


Interpreting Monitoring
When remote sources are involved, troubleshooting should consider the full end-to-end data access path rather than only local database activity.


Support Scope Clarification
When analyzing statements accessing virtual tables, the involved components may include:

  • SAP HANA database
  • network communication
  • remote data source

Observed behavior alone is not sufficient to determine a root cause and requires end-to-end analysis across all involved components.


Conclusion
SAP HANA Smart Data Access allows access to external data without replication.
Virtual tables represent remote objects, and data access from the connected system is part of normal execution behavior.
Statements executed in SAP HANA may therefore include processing steps outside the SAP HANA database, while SAP HANA operates according to its documented architecture.
Understanding this behavior supports correct interpretation during troubleshooting.


Disclaimer
This article summarizes documented system behavior based on publicly available SAP documentation and SAP Knowledge Base Articles.

Only official SAP documentation and SAP Notes are authoritative.

entry.title: Re: How to create custom fields with supplier bank data (IBAN & BIC) in next-gen Ariba Invoicing
entry.content:
Hello @stdo

You don't seem to be on Public Cloud.

Displaying the supplier IBAN/BIC in that popup would not be solved through standard S4H Cloud supplier master configuration.

Adding custom fields in Ariba Invoicing may allow additional invoice fields, but those fields appear to be input or invoice data fields, not automatically derived supplier master fields.

If your requirement is for invoice validation, the better route would be to raise this as an SAP Ariba enhancement request or check with the support team whether a customer-specific Ariba extension or integration option is available.

 

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: [S/4HANA Private Cloud] Flexible Workflow – Sales Ord...
entry.content:
Given your constraints that pricing not persisted before commit, SELECT forbidden, the practical recommendation is

  • Stick with your current “launch always, evaluate later” design.
  • Add a final auto-release step in the workflow definition that runs if no approval step was executed.
  • This way, the workflow itself cleans up the status, and you don’t need to hack BAdIs to reset approval.

Having said the above, your next step should be

  • In Manage Workflows for Sales Orders, add a final step with action = releaseSalesOrder.
  • Condition: “NOT ZP06” (your custom evaluator).
  • Test:
    • With ZP06 → approval step runs, release after approval.
    • Without ZP06 → approval skipped, final step releases order.

entry.title: Re: Role Management Strategy in EWM Implementation Projec...
entry.content:
The recommended pattern is

  1. Build roles in DEV → Transport to QA for testing → Transport to PROD for consistency.
  2. Assign only the required roles in PROD; keep others unassigned until needed.
  3. Document role ownership and usage so that during audits or refreshes, you know exactly what’s safe to keep or remove.
  4. Use composite roles or business role concepts in EWM to group module-specific roles for easier assignment.
